name: Area — Counting the Space Inside
whatIsIt: Area is how much flat space a shape covers INSIDE its boundary. We measure it by counting unit squares — like tiles on a floor. A rectangle's tiles sit in neat rows, so instead of counting one by one: rows × tiles-per-row. Area of a rectangle = length × breadth, in SQUARE units.
whyNeeded: How many tiles for the bathroom floor? How much paint for the wall? How big is the farm? Whenever you cover, paint, or plant a SURFACE, you're buying area. Perimeter walks the edge; area fills the inside — mixing them up buys the wrong amount of everything!
realLifeUses: Tiling: how many square tiles cover the kitchen floor?
Painting: how much wall does one paint tin cover?
Farming: how big is the field to be planted?
Wrapping: is this paper sheet big enough for the gift box?

vocabulary.meaning: The amount of flat space inside a shape's boundary.
A 1-by-1 square — the tile we count area with.
Area's units: square cm, square m — because we count SQUARES.

story.title: Tiling the Wedding Mandap
story.text: The wedding mandap floor is 6 tiles long and 4 tiles wide. 'How many tiles cover it completely?' asks Ravi. The tile-layer sets them in 4 equal rows of 6. 'Count the array: area = 6 × 4 = 24 square tiles. Edges you ADD for perimeter; the inside you MULTIPLY for area.'
story.extractedProblem: A dance floor is 6 tiles long and 4 tiles wide. How many tiles cover it completely?
story.answerInStory: 24 tiles — 4 equal rows of 6: area = 6 × 4 = 24 square tiles.

visual.caption: Count the unit squares: 4 rows of 6 = 24. That's why area of a rectangle = length × breadth.

teachingGallery.title: Area — the space inside
teachingGallery.note: Cover it in unit squares: rows × columns.

standardMethod.summary: Rectangle area = length × breadth, answered in square units.
steps: Check it's a rectangle (or split the shape into rectangles).
Say WHY it works: the unit squares sit in 4 equal rows of 6 — an array!
Answer in SQUARE units: 24 square metres (not just 'metres').

alternateMethods.name: Count-the-squares method
alternateMethods.whenToUse: First meetings with area, and odd shapes on grid paper.
steps: Draw the shape on squared paper.
Count every full square inside the boundary.
For rectangles, notice the rows are equal — multiplication is just fast counting!
alternateMethods.example: An L-shaped room on grid paper: count squares one by one, or split into two rectangles.

alternateMethods.name: Split-into-rectangles method
steps: Cut the shape into rectangles with one straight line.
Add the pieces — just like partial products!
alternateMethods.example: An L = a 4×3 rectangle + a 2×2 square → 12 + 4 = 16 square units.

workedExamples.problem: A classroom notice board is 3 m long and 2 m wide. What is its area?
steps: Units: 6 SQUARE metres — six 1m×1m squares of chart paper would exactly cover it.

workedExamples.problem: Two gardens: A is 6 m × 4 m, B is 8 m × 3 m. Ravi says B is bigger 'because 8 is the biggest number'. Who's right?
steps: A: 6 × 4 = 24 square m. B: 8 × 3 = 24 square m.
They're exactly EQUAL — different shapes can hold the same space. One long-and-thin, one short-and-wide.

trickPractice.intro: Area is how many unit squares fit inside — just multiply length by width.

questions.q: A rectangle is 4 by 3. What is its area (in squares)?
hintLadder: Area = cover it in unit squares.

questions.q: A rectangle is 6 by 2. What is its area (in squares)?
hintLadder: Area = cover it in unit squares.

questions.q: A rectangle is 5 by 5. What is its area (in squares)?
hintLadder: Area = cover it in unit squares.

questions.q: A rectangle is 7 by 3. What is its area (in squares)?
hintLadder: Area = cover it in unit squares.

questions.q: A rectangle is 8 by 2. What is its area (in squares)?
hintLadder: Area = cover it in unit squares.

questions.q: A rectangle is 4 by 4. What is its area (in squares)?
hintLadder: Area = cover it in unit squares.

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding the sides (6+4+6+4=20) when asked for area — buying the border instead of the floor!
commonMistakes.fix: Ask: edge question or inside question? Fencing/ribbon = edge = ADD. Tiles/paint/planting = inside = MULTIPLY. Remember Ravi's hollow dance floor!

commonMistakes.mistake: Writing area as '24 metres' instead of 24 SQUARE metres.
commonMistakes.fix: Area counts SQUARES, so its units are square units: square cm, square m. '24 metres' is a length — a walk, not a floor.

commonMistakes.mistake: Judging which shape is bigger by its longest side instead of computing areas.
commonMistakes.fix: A long thin strip can hold LESS than a chubby square! Always multiply l × b for each shape, then compare the areas.

easy.q: A chocolate bar is 5 squares long and 3 squares wide. How many small squares does it have (its area)?
hintLadder: The squares sit in 3 equal rows.

easy.q: Tiles for a floor is an ______ question; fence for a garden is an ______ question.
hintLadder: Where do tiles go — around or all over?
Inside = area = multiply; edge = perimeter = add.

medium.q: A courtyard is 9 m long and 4 m wide. How many square metres of tiles cover it?
The area of a 7 cm × 2 cm card is written correctly as…

challenge.q: An L-shaped room splits into a 4 m × 3 m rectangle and a 2 m × 2 m square. Total area in square metres?
Garden A is 10 m × 2 m. Garden B is 5 m × 5 m. Which holds more planting space?

options.label: B — 25 square m beats A's 20 square m
A — it has the longest side (10)

challenge.answer: B — 25 square m beats A's 20 square m

questions.q: Area of an 8 m × 3 m rectangle, in square metres?

hintLadder: What do we count for area?
So the units are square units.

questions.q: For the SAME 6 × 4 rectangle: perimeter and area are…

options.label: Perimeter 20 (add all sides), area 24 (multiply) — different things!

questions.answer: Perimeter 20 (add all sides), area 24 (multiply) — different things!
hintLadder: The walk and the floor are different measurements.

questions.q: A square stamp has 4 cm sides. Its area in square cm?
hintLadder: A square's length and breadth are the same.

teachBackPrompt: Retell Ravi's hollow dance floor disaster in your own words: why did 20 tiles only make a border, why did 6 × 4 fix it, and what's the one question ('edge or inside?') that keeps perimeter and area apart?

revisionCard.summary: Area = space INSIDE = count unit squares = length × breadth (rows of tiles = an array!). Units: SQUARE cm/m. Edge questions (fence/ribbon) ADD sides = perimeter; inside questions (tiles/paint) MULTIPLY = area. Odd shapes: split into rectangles and add.

gameMission.title: The Dance Floor Rescue
gameMission.brief: Floors all over Shape City need tiling before the festival! Order exact tile counts by multiplying, split the tricky L-shaped halls, and reject Ravi's border-only orders before another hollow floor!

realLifeProject: Tile inspector: pick a tiled floor or wall at home. Count the tiles in one row and the number of rows, multiply for the total, then spot-check by counting a small patch. Report your floor's area in tiles!

rememberIt.hook: Area = space inside (square units). Count or multiply squares
rememberIt.unpack: Rectangle area = length x breadth, measured in square units (cm^2). Different from perimeter (the edge).

studentQuestions.a: Area is the space INSIDE (square units); perimeter is the distance AROUND (length units).
Because we count how many unit SQUARES fit inside - so cm^2, m^2, etc.
